section.title: Dinah
section.sectionRationale: After Jacob has met God at Peniel and reconciled with Esau, this dark chapter shows the danger of settling near Shechem. Dinah is violated, and her brothers answer treachery with treachery, leaving a trail of blood that Jacob will still be lamenting on his deathbed.
section.thesis: When God's people mingle carelessly with the world and take justice into their own hands by deceit, the result is defilement, deception, and slaughter; yet the chapter exposes the deep need for a righteousness the sons of Jacob do not have.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the violation of Dinah; the deceitful bargain over circumcision; the massacre at Shechem; and Jacob's fearful rebuke answered by his sons.

verses.text: Now Dinah, the daughter Leah had borne to Jacob, went out to visit the daughters of the land.
When Shechem son of Hamor the Hivite, the prince of the region, saw her, he took her and lay with her by force.
And his soul was drawn to Dinah, the daughter of Jacob. He loved the young girl and spoke to her tenderly.
So Shechem told his father Hamor, “Get me this girl as a wife.”
Jacob heard that Shechem had {{tame|defiled}} his daughter Dinah, but since his sons were with his livestock in the field, he remained silent about it until they returned.
Meanwhile, Shechem’s father Hamor came to speak with Jacob.

When Jacob’s sons heard what had happened, they returned from the field. They were filled with grief and fury, because Shechem had committed an {{nebalah|outrage}} in Israel by lying with Jacob’s daughter, a thing that should not be done.
But Hamor said to them, “My son Shechem longs for your daughter. Please give her to him as his wife.
Intermarry with us; give us your daughters and take our daughters for yourselves.
You may settle among us, and the land will be open to you. Live here, move about freely, and acquire your own property.”
Then Shechem said to Dinah’s father and brothers, “Grant me this favor, and I will give you whatever you ask.
Demand a high dowry and an expensive gift, and I will give you whatever you ask. Only give me the girl as my wife!”
But because Shechem had defiled their sister Dinah, Jacob’s sons answered him and his father Hamor {{mirmah|deceitfully}}.
“We cannot do such a thing,” they said. “To give our sister to an uncircumcised man would be a disgrace to us.
We will consent to this on one condition, that you become circumcised like us, every one of your males.
Then we will give you our daughters and take your daughters for ourselves. We will dwell among you and become one people.
But if you will not agree to be circumcised, then we will take our sister and go.”
Their offer seemed good to Hamor and his son Shechem.
The young man, who was the most respected of all his father’s household, did not hesitate to fulfill this request, because he was delighted with Jacob’s daughter.
So Hamor and his son Shechem went to the gate of their city and addressed the men of their city:
“These men are at peace with us. Let them live and trade in our land; indeed, it is large enough for them. Let us take their daughters in marriage and give our daughters to them.
But only on this condition will the men agree to dwell with us and be one people: if all our men are circumcised as they are.
Will not their livestock, their possessions, and all their animals become ours? Only let us consent to them, and they will dwell among us.”
All the men who went out of the city gate listened to Hamor and his son Shechem, and every male of the city was circumcised.

units.label: The massacre at Shechem
units.note: Simeon and Levi with the sword

verses.text: Three days later, while they were still in pain, two of Jacob’s sons (Dinah’s brothers Simeon and Levi) took their swords, went into the unsuspecting city, and slaughtered every male.
They killed Hamor and his son Shechem with their swords, took Dinah out of Shechem’s house, and went away.
Jacob’s other sons came upon the slaughter and looted the city, because their sister had been defiled.
They took their flocks and herds and donkeys, and everything else in the city or in the field.
They carried off all their possessions and women and children, and they plundered everything in their houses.

Then Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, “You have brought {{akar|trouble}} upon me by making me a stench to the Canaanites and Perizzites, the people of this land. We are few in number; if they unite against me and attack me, I and my household will be destroyed.”
But they replied, “Should he have treated our sister like a prostitute?”

tame.gloss: To make unclean or defile. The verb runs like a refrain through the chapter, verses 5, 13, and 27, naming Shechem's act not merely as passion but as a violation that pollutes. It is the same word used for ritual and moral uncleanness, marking Dinah as sinned against, not shamed by her own doing.

nebalah.gloss: A disgraceful outrage, a wicked folly that tears the fabric of a community. It is used elsewhere for the worst violations of covenant order, such as sexual crimes in Israel. The narrator's use here signals that what happened to Dinah is not a private matter but an assault on the whole people God is forming.

mirmah.gloss: Deceit or treachery. This is the very word associated with Jacob's own grasping, when Isaac says his brother came with deceit, Genesis 27:35. Now his sons answer a wrong with the family sin of deception, showing how deceit is passed down and used to cloak murder in the language of covenant and circumcision.

akar.gloss: To bring trouble, to stir up ruin like muddied water. Jacob fears his sons have made him a stench who could be wiped out. The same root marks Achan, who troubled Israel, Joshua 7:25, tying reckless sin to danger for the whole household of faith.

basic.context: Jacob has just met God and reconciled with Esau, but instead of pressing on to Bethel he settles near the city of Shechem. His only daughter, Dinah, goes out and is violated by the local prince. Her brothers, especially Simeon and Levi, take terrible revenge. It is one of the darkest chapters in Genesis.
basic.hermeneutics: The key question is who, if anyone, acts rightly here. The honest answer is almost no one. The narrator reports the events without praising the brothers; their deceit and slaughter are told plainly and later condemned. The Bible often shows us sin without approving it, so we read for warning, not for a model.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter repeats the word defiled to name the wrong done to Dinah, and it uses outrage, a word for wicked folly that damages the whole community. The brothers answer with deceit, the very family sin of Jacob, and Jacob complains that they have brought trouble on him, a word that means to stir up ruin.
basic.typology: The sons of Jacob show they cannot bring true justice; they only add sin to sin. This exposes the need for a righteous Judge and Deliverer who will one day come from this very family, though not from Simeon or Levi. The scattering of Levi is later turned to blessing when God sets them apart for Himself.
basic.christ: This chapter drives us to long for the true Son of Jacob, Jesus, who neither defiles nor deceives, and who brings a justice that heals rather than destroys. Where the brothers avenge with the sword, Christ absorbs violence and turns even the tribe of Levi into servants of the sanctuary.
basic.god: God is not named as acting in this chapter, yet His moral order stands behind every line. The violation is a real wrong; the vengeance is real sin. God's silence is not His absence; He is patiently working out His purposes even through a compromised family.
basic.crossReferences: Jacob's deathbed words in Genesis 49 curse the cruelty of Simeon and Levi. Deuteronomy names such acts an outrage in Israel. Romans warns against repaying evil for evil and taking revenge into our own hands.
basic.oneStory: This is a low point in the family chosen to bless the world. It shows how deeply sin has infected even the covenant line, and why the story must keep moving toward a Redeemer who can do what Jacob's sons cannot: bring justice and mercy together.

context.literary: Set within the toledot of Jacob, the chapter interrupts the journey to Bethel that God commanded. Its placement, right after reconciliation with Esau and right before the return to Bethel in chapter 35, frames it as the danger of lingering and settling among the Canaanites rather than following God's call.
context.historical: Shechem was a real Canaanite city in the central hill country. Circumcision was practiced by some peoples but here is demanded as a covenant sign, then cynically weaponized. Intermarriage with the Canaanites threatened the distinct identity of the family through whom God had promised to bless all nations.
context.canonical: The episode reverberates through the canon: Genesis 49:5-7 pronounces judgment on Simeon and Levi, and the later scattering of both tribes fulfills it, though Levi's scattering is redeemed into priestly service. It stands as a case study of vengeance that Scripture will contrast with God's justice.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Moses records this to warn Israel against careless assimilation with the surrounding nations and against private vengeance, and to show honestly that the chosen family is riddled with sin and in need of grace and a coming Redeemer.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes deceit and slaughter; it does not prescribe them. The absence of divine approval, and the later curse in Genesis 49, make clear that the brothers' response is condemned, even though the original crime against Dinah was genuinely evil.

debates.question: Were Simeon and Levi's actions a defensible defense of their sister, or sinful vengeance?

views.label: Sinful vengeance
views.case: The narrator stresses their deceit, the treacherous use of the covenant sign, the killing of an entire unsuspecting city, and the plunder. Jacob's later curse in Genesis 49:5-7 settles the matter: their anger was cruel and their wrath fierce.

views.label: Understandable zeal for Dinah
views.case: Their closing question, should he have treated our sister like a prostitute, voices real moral outrage at a crime Jacob seemed slow to address, and some read them as flawed men reacting to genuine injustice.

debates.bearing: Both agree a true wrong was done to Dinah. The text and canon judge the brothers' means as sinful, teaching that righteous anger at injustice must not become deceit and slaughter; justice belongs finally to God.

study.originalLanguages: The narrator frames the crime with tame, defiled, repeated so we never lose sight of the wrong done to Dinah, and with nebalah, an outrage or wicked folly that damages the whole community. The brothers reply with mirmah, deceit, the very word tied to Jacob's own grasping, showing sin handed down through the family. Jacob's protest that they have brought akar, trouble, upon him uses a word for stirring up ruin, hinting that unchecked violence endangers the whole covenant household.

typology.type: The failed justice of Jacob's sons
typology.fulfillment: Points forward to Christ, the righteous Judge from Judah's line, who brings a justice that saves rather than merely destroys.

typology.type: The scattering of Levi under judgment
typology.fulfillment: Turned by grace into the priestly tribe set apart for God, a shadow of how God redeems even judgment, ultimately in Christ our High Priest.

study.christ: The chapter shows a family unable to answer evil without multiplying it, and so it makes us ache for a better Son of Jacob. Jesus, descended from this very household through Judah, meets defilement without being defiled, faces betrayal without deceit, and bears violence rather than dealing it. Where Simeon and Levi take up the sword and are cursed for it, Christ takes the curse on Himself. Even the tribe of Levi, scattered in judgment here, is gathered into priestly service, foreshadowing the great High Priest who reconciles God and sinners.
study.god: Though God is never named as acting in the chapter, His law and character govern it: the violation of Dinah is a genuine evil, and the deceitful slaughter is genuine sin. His seeming silence is not indifference. He is the God who sees the abused and who will judge both the crime and the cruel vengeance, working patiently to preserve and purify the family through whom He will bless the world.

crossReferences.note: Jacob curses the violence of Simeon and Levi and foretells their scattering in Israel.
Uses the same language of outrage in Israel for grave sexual sin, showing the seriousness of the crime against Dinah.
Do not repay evil for evil; leave room for God's wrath, for vengeance belongs to the Lord.
God sets apart the scattered tribe of Levi for Himself, redeeming judgment into priestly service.

study.oneStory: This chapter is a shadowed valley in the story of the family God chose to bless the nations. It shows how far sin has spread even among the sons of Jacob, and why the promise cannot rest on their righteousness. The line must be carried by grace toward the one righteous Son, Jesus, who will bring the justice and mercy that Simeon and Levi could not, and heal what their swords could only break.

quiz.q: How does the narrator want us to view Simeon and Levi's slaughter of the Shechemites?

options.text: As sinful vengeance, told without approval and later cursed by Jacob.
options.note: The deceit, the massacre, and Genesis 49:5-7 make the judgment clear.

options.text: As understandable but excessive, reacting to a real crime against Dinah.
options.note: It captures the real outrage, but understates that Scripture condemns the means.

options.text: As a neutral report with no moral weight either way.
options.note: The chapter is heavy with moral weight; it is not neutral.

options.text: As righteous justice that God commanded and blessed.
options.note: God neither commands nor blesses it; Jacob later curses it.

quiz.q: What was the crime that set the chapter in motion?

options.text: Shechem seized and violated Dinah, an outrage against her and the family.
options.note: The text names it defilement and an outrage in Israel.

options.text: Dinah married outside the family without permission.
options.note: Intermarriage is a theme, but the crime is the violation, not a marriage.

options.text: Jacob settled too close to a Canaanite city.
options.note: This was unwise and set the stage, but it is not the crime itself.

options.text: The brothers stole livestock from Shechem.
options.note: The plunder came later as part of their vengeance, not the initial crime.

quiz.q: How did the sons of Jacob turn the covenant sign of circumcision into a weapon?

options.text: They demanded it deceitfully, then attacked the men while they were in pain.
options.note: They cloaked murder in the language of covenant, verses 13 to 25.

options.text: They refused to be circumcised themselves to insult the Shechemites.
options.note: The reverse; they were circumcised and demanded it of others as a trap.

options.text: They circumcised the Shechemites to make them true worshipers.
options.note: Their aim was not conversion but incapacitation and slaughter.

options.text: They ignored circumcision entirely in the bargain.
options.note: Circumcision was the very center of their deceitful condition.

quiz.q: Where does this dark chapter point in the larger story of the Bible?

options.text: To the need for a righteous Redeemer from Jacob's line who brings true justice.
options.note: The family's failure makes us long for Christ, the true Son of Jacob.

options.text: To the idea that God had abandoned Jacob's family for good.
options.note: God is patient here, not absent; He keeps working through the flawed line.

options.text: To a lesson that revenge usually works out well in the end.
options.note: The chapter shows revenge sowing fear and ruin, not success.

options.text: To proof that only Simeon and Levi were faithful.
options.note: They are the ones Jacob later curses, not commends.

quiz.q: What is Jacob's stated concern in his rebuke, and what is missing from it?

options.text: He fears retaliation and ruin, saying little of the wrong done or God's justice.
options.note: His focus is on danger to himself and his household, verse 30.

options.text: He grieves deeply over the murdered Shechemites by name.
options.note: There is fear more than lament in his words; he names his own peril.

options.text: He praises his sons for defending Dinah's honor.
options.note: He does not praise them; he rebukes them for the danger they caused.

options.text: He immediately leads the family in repentance and worship.
options.note: That call to purify and worship comes only in the next chapter, at Bethel.

questions: This chapter refuses to hide the sins of God's chosen family. How does it comfort you, or unsettle you, that the Bible tells the truth about its heroes?
Dinah is sinned against, yet Scripture never blames her. How can the church better see and defend those who have been wronged, rather than shaming them?
The brothers had real reason for anger but chose deceit and violence. Where might righteous anger be tempting you toward sinful means, and what would trusting God's justice look like instead?
Jacob's compromise in settling near Shechem set the scene for disaster. Are there places where a slow drift from God's call is exposing your household to harm?
The chapter leaves us longing for a better Son of Jacob. How does Jesus, who meets evil without multiplying it, reshape the way you respond to those who wrong you?

translationNotes.note: The phrase committed an outrage in Israel can also be rendered against Israel; either way it names the act as a grave violation of the covenant community, not a private matter.
Spoke to her tenderly is literally spoke to the heart of the young woman; the tenderness of Shechem's later feelings does not undo the violence of verse 2.
The brothers' closing question is left unanswered in the text, a deliberate narrative gap that invites the reader to weigh both the real wrong done to Dinah and the sinfulness of the vengeance.
